title: RBA walks the inflation tightrope with rate hold
full_text:
Australians will suffer through an economic slowdown and rising unemployment over the next few months as the Reserve Bank moves to strangle dangerously high inflation in an already battered economy.
The RBA on Tuesday held the benchmark cash rate steady at 4.35 per cent – its highest level in years – and stated bluntly that it was deliberately pushing for an economic slowdown to reduce price pressures.
“We are forecasting that growth is going to slow and growth has got to slow … that is what is needed to try and get inflation down,” RBA governor Michele Bullock said.
“People shouldn’t be alarmed about that – that’s what has got to happen in order to bring inflation down.” Inflation is running at 4.2 per cent, well above the RBA’s target of 2-3 per cent.
Earlier this month, the Australian Bureau of Statistics reported anaemic GDP growth of just 0.3 per cent in the March quarter, down from 0.9 per cent in the previous quarter.
In April, unemployment ticked up to 4.5 per cent.
Ms Bullock said the RBA did not want to tip the economy into a recession, but needed to “slow it enough” to bring inflation back down to 2.5 per cent.
NAB chief economist Sally Auld said she expected growth to slow to 1.5 per cent by the end of the year and unemployment would lift to close to 5 per cent over the next 18 months.
“But that’s not a recession,” she said. “It’s pretty slow growth and that’s the necessary outcome that you need.
“If you don’t have that, inflation won’t come back into the target band.” Dr Auld warned conditions would get “more challenging” for regular households and businesses in the near term.
“It’s not a very happy set of forecasts,” she said.
“The economy is going to slow, the unemployment rate is going to go up … that’s not an easy place to be whether you’re running a business or managing a household budget.
“But the silver lining in all of this is that if we get the slowdown, that is not a recession.
“If we get a mild slowing in growth and a modest adjustment in the labour market – if that’s successful and we get inflation back into the target band – then that on its own will really help households.
“High inflation hurts everyone.” The RBA has already hit Aussie homeowners with three rate hikes this year, adding hundreds of dollars to monthly mortgage payments, and yesterday’s decision to hold offers some breathing space for homeowners.
But the relief could be short-lived.
KPMG chief economist Brendan Rynne warned a fourth rates hike could still come in August.
“Higher diesel and fertiliser costs from the Iran war are now flowing through and worsening food and transport prices, which will not help in the inflation fight,” he said.
“For this reason, we can expect at least another hike this year, most likely in August, in order to bring core inflation back down to the midpoint of the RBA’s target band.” In its statement explaining yesterday’s hold, the RBA board said it remained focused on ensuring “inflation does not become embedded once the impulse from higher oil prices has passed through”.
“To achieve this, growth in demand needs to slow,” the statement read. Treasurer Jim Chalmers hailed the decision as a “welcome reprieve” for Australians with a mortgage.
“It’s a reassuring result in the face of extreme volatility in the global economy,” he said. Editorial Page 29
